How Retrieval Practice Strengthens Reading Memory

Retrieval practice strengthens reading memory because the learner must rebuild the idea from cues instead of merely recognizing familiar sentences. The act of reconstructing the claim exposes missing links, makes attention measurable, and gives the next reread a concrete job.

A useful companion waits until the reader has had time to inspect the paragraph, then asks for the causal mechanism in the reader's own words. A weak answer usually repeats the topic while missing the mechanism: rebuilding from cues reveals gaps and guides the next pass.

The policy should prefer a quiet checkpoint over constant interruption. When the moment is right, one question is enough to reveal whether the section was skimmed, understood, or needs another pass.
